text: Robotic posterior exenteration and partial vaginectomy with bilateral transverse gracilis flap vaginal reconstruction for locally advanced rectal cancer. The patient is a forty-nine-year-old female who presented with abdominal pain with hematochesia. Computer tomography and subsequent colonoscopy revealed a circumferential rectal mass extending from the anal verge to midrectum up to fifteen centimeters. Biopsy revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Although coexisting gynecological malignancy was suspected, given the size of the mass and the proximity to the uterus, cervical biopsy revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma concerning for the rectal cancer. Rectal MRI suggested extensive involvement of vagina and cervix with perirectal nodal involvement without any distant metastasis. She underwent H-cycle of neoadjuvant furfox followed by a fifty-degree of chemoradiation. She achieved complete clinical response on rectal MRI, flexor sigmoidoscopy, and the clinical exam. Although she was offered posterior exenteration at that time, she elected close surveillance without surgery. However, she developed luminal recurrence on flexor sigmoidoscopy in a year, for which she was offered posterior pelvic exenteration and partial vaginotomy with vaginal reconstruction with bilateral transverse gracious flap for salvage.

Under general anesthesia in lithotomy position, the DaVinci XI robotic system is used with four robotic arms and two assistant ports. The surgeon marks the colostomy site and docks the robot between the legs. The peritoneum medial to the right iliac vessels is incised, and the mesentery is mobilized from the retroperitoneum using a medial approach. The left ureter is identified during the procedure.

The surgeon continues dissection medially behind the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and initiates lymphadenectomy at the IMA origin. The dissection progresses laterally to connect with the previously performed medial dissection. The lymphadenectomy is completed, and the superior rectal artery and left colic artery are identified.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler with a vascular load to transect the superior rectal artery. The colonic mesentery is divided with a robotic vessel sealer, and the sigmoid colon is transected using a robotic stapler, freeing the upper specimen.

The surgeon performs posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) within the mesorectum envelope, dissecting down to the pelvic floor. The rectum is mobilized circumferentially along the right and left rectal walls, with visible fibrotic tissues from previous radiation. The dissection proceeds anteriorly to remove the uterus and vagina en bloc with the rectum, assisted by the gynecology oncology team.

The surgeon divides the round ligaments bilaterally and opens the retroperitoneal space. The infundibulopelvic ligaments are isolated, coagulated, and divided bilaterally. The bladder is dissected down anteriorly by approximately four to five centimeters. The uterine vessels are skeletonized, coagulated, and divided. A uterine manipulator is inserted into the endometrial cavity before the incision, and additional bites of the cardinal ligaments are taken to the uterine manipulator. The surgeon continues to separate the vagina from the bladder distally, ensuring both ureters remain intact.
The surgeon transects the levator muscles in the right, left, and posterior planes, followed by further dissection of the anterior plane between the bladder and vagina.

The uterine manipulator is removed, and the robotic system is undocked. The surgeon makes an elliptical incision around the perianal skin and the posterior right and left walls of the vagina. Dissection proceeds into the ischioanal and ischiorectal spaces. The focus then shifts to reconstructing the vaginal and perineal wounds.

A longitudinal incision is made through the left medial posterior thigh to identify the gracilis muscle. The muscle is divided at its distal portion and elevated from its bed in a distal to proximal fashion. A counterincision is created, and the muscle is transposed into the perineal defect. The distal ends of the two muscles are sutured together, and the muscle flaps are inverted into the perineal wound and secured to the surrounding tissue. A specimen picture shows the quality of the total mesorectal excision (TME) with no evidence of grossly positive margins.

Here is the evidence of the direct invasion to the vagina. The procedure took eleven hours, with robotic portion being five hours. Estimated blood loss was three hundred ml. The patient was discharged on the post-operative day eight with Foley catheter for urinary retention. Final pathology revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with no lymph node metastasis. All margins were negative. The patient is disease-free for almost two years.

In conclusion, this video demonstrates that robotic assisted posterior accentuation and partial vaginotomy with transverse gracilis flap vaginal reconstruction is technically feasible under a multidisciplinary surgical team.
